video_title: How To Invest In OpenAI and Anthropic Before They Go Public
gist: Ankur Nagpal (USVC GP, ex-Teachable founder, now at AngelList) makes the case for a small private-markets sleeve on top of an index portfolio and pitches USVC, a publicly accessible, $500-minimum, NAV-based tender-offer venture fund holding SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI and others. Despite the title, the episode is a broad private-markets/secondaries/tax-alpha conversation rather than a specific guide to buying OpenAI or Anthropic; the labs' likely IPOs are discussed only briefly.

insights.claim: Ankur Nagpal argues private markets should be only a 10-20% 'asymmetric bet' sleeve layered on top of a portfolio that is 70-90% indexed, not a replacement for public-market indexing.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Sets the sizing discipline for retail allocating to any private-market vehicle; treat venture exposure as tail-upside capital you can afford to lock up.
insights.quote: if you have 70, 80, 90% indexing the market, you can afford to take asymmetric bets with the leftover 10, 15, 20%

insights.claim: The historical case for venture is eroding: reported average venture returns of ~17-18% versus a NASDAQ of ~12-13% imply ~400bps outperformance, but once inflated paper marks are discounted the real edge may be closer to ~100bps, which may not justify locking up capital.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If venture's illiquidity premium is really only ~100bps, LPs should demand much cheaper access or better selection to bother locking up capital.
insights.quote: maybe the outperformance is really like a 100 basis points and maybe that's not worth me locking up my capital

insights.claim: The number of public companies in America has fallen by roughly half and companies now go public on average after ~13 years, so most wealth compounding has migrated into private markets and is inaccessible to ordinary investors.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Structural driver behind demand for retail private-market products; the public-market menu is shrinking while value creation happens pre-IPO.
insights.quote: the number of public companies in America has fallen by half. Companies are going public on average after 13 years

insights.claim: Retail investors doing direct angel/private deals face structural adverse selection unless they live in San Francisco or New York and are plugged into specific tech communities, because talent concentrates in a few magnet cities.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Undercuts DIY angel investing for outsiders; argues for pooled/indexed access rather than 'your cousin's startup' deal flow.
insights.quote: by default they're getting adverse selection because it's their their friend's kid who's starting a company

insights.claim: Nagpal says he personally holds more than half his net worth in private markets while explicitly telling listeners not to do that, framing private markets as suitable only after 401k match, Roth IRA and HSA are maxed and the index core is built.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A candid conflict-of-interest tell from a fund GP; the operator eats his own cooking but explicitly warns retail against replicating the concentration.
insights.quote: Personally I'm very responsible. I have more than half my money in private markets. I do not think people should do that.

insights.claim: Nagpal assigns a very high probability that at least one frontier AI lab goes public later this year, and says an Anthropic IPO later this year is 'very likely' and would make it one of the fastest companies to IPO in recent history.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A near-term catalyst watch for AI-exposed private vehicles and secondaries; a lab IPO would reprice private marks across the sector.
insights.quote: there's a very high probability we will see at least one of the frontier labs going out later this year

insights.claim: Nagpal frames the LLM labs' pledge to give ~5% of equity to the government 'for the American people' as effectively an admission that they should just go public, since a listed company would let anyone buy in.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'nationalized equity' narrative as a symptom of companies staying private too long; watch whether public pressure accelerates IPO timelines.
insights.quote: this like nationalized control or nationalized interest for the American people is actually a exhaust of the fact that the companies aren't public

insights.claim: Pompliano's opening thesis is that public resentment of AI stems from ordinary Americans being locked out of its wealth creation, and that labs are starting to share equity to improve sentiment toward the technology.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Political-economy lens on why access products and equity-sharing schemes are emerging; sentiment risk is a real variable for AI-heavy portfolios.
insights.quote: the average American is fully locked out of that wealth creation

insights.claim: Nagpal expects a diversification of IPO strategy among labs: there is a benefit to going first, but whoever goes second will closely watch the first listing's reception before proceeding.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Implies the first lab IPO becomes a pricing/reception benchmark that gates the rest; the first print is the one to trade around.
insights.quote: there's a benefit to going first. Um, however, whoever goes second will very closely watch what happens to the first one

themes.theme: USVC fund structure vs closed-end ETFs

insights.claim: USVC is a fully publicly accessible venture fund open to any American with as little as $500 (no accreditation required), structured as a tender-offer fund targeting up to 5% liquidity of the fund per quarter at NAV rather than indefinite lockup.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Defines the actual product on offer; the 5%/quarter tender cap is the key liquidity constraint investors must underwrite before assuming they can exit.
insights.quote: open to anyone in America with as little as $500... our goal is to give liquidity or offer liquidity every quarter. It's not guaranteed... of up to 5% of the entire fund

insights.claim: USVC transacts subscriptions and redemptions at NAV, which Nagpal contrasts with closed-end vehicles that decouple from underlying value: he cites Destiny trading at nearly a 100x premium and Robinhood's vehicle trading at ~2x NAV.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: The NAV-transaction design is USVC's core differentiator; it caps the premium/discount blowups that plague listed private-access ETFs.
insights.quote: there was a ETF by this company called Destiny. They at one point they were trading at almost 100 times premium to what's underneath

insights.claim: USVC creates new shares each time an investor buys in at NAV (currently ~$20 and change), giving the fund fresh capital to deploy, rather than monetizing a premium the way premium-trading closed-end vehicles issue shares.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Clarifies the capital-raising mechanics; inflows directly fund new deals, aligning fund growth with deployment capacity.

themes.theme: SPV opacity, the Anduril dispute, and fraud

insights.claim: USVC acquired its Anduril stake by buying out LPs in an existing SPV that sits on the cap table, not directly from the company; Nagpal concedes he mishandled the public announcement, prompting co-founder Matt Grimm to publicly dispute that USVC had 'invested in' Anduril.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Illustrates the reputational and disclosure risk in secondary/SPV access; how a stake is described publicly can trigger company pushback even when the transaction is legitimate.
insights.quote: we did eventually buy a stake in Andrew, but it was by buying out LPS and an SPV

insights.claim: USVC has a blanket policy never to buy SPVs that are invested in other SPVs, because multi-layer SPVs on AngelList only reveal another LLC underneath with no way to see the ultimate asset.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A concrete diligence red-line for anyone buying private secondaries; nested SPVs hide fees, ownership, and sometimes the absence of any real asset.
insights.quote: we have a blanket policy that we're going to not buy SPVS that are invested in other SPVS

insights.claim: The all-in fee load on some SPV exposure can reach 10-20% (Nagpal cites seeing a 10% management fee and stacked 2%/20% plus additional 3% layers), yet buyers pay it because they believe they can't access the name any other way.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how much fee leakage can silently destroy private-market returns; access scarcity is being used to justify predatory economics.
insights.quote: I've seen one time 10% management fee... the full fee load on an exposure sometimes could be as much as 10, 15, 20%

insights.claim: Nagpal warns the most terrifying private-market risk is SPVs where there is literally nothing underneath (outright fraud), and that 'forward contracts' promising future equity delivery may be unenforceable once the seller has made millions and lawyered up.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Flags enforceability risk in pre-IPO forward contracts as an under-appreciated failure mode; buyers may hold paper promises with no legal recourse.
insights.quote: the most terrifying part is sometimes there's nothing underneath

insights.claim: Nagpal describes brokers pitching private-company exposure using stale or fabricated financials (e.g. 2021 numbers presented as current), which can turn rumors into de facto 'fact' in investors' minds and create problems for the underlying company.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Explains why companies like Anduril guard their cap tables; investors should demand current, sourced financials before buying any private secondary.

insights.claim: AngelList's ~13-14 years of data show investors with a high sample size of deals cluster at ~12-15% IRR (up to ~20% for the best), while individuals doing only a handful of deals face a crapshoot where all can go to zero, so 'shots on goal' is the dominant driver of outcomes.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Empirical backbone for an index approach to venture; concentration in 3-5 private names is closer to gambling than investing.
insights.quote: if you invest in five deals they can all go to zero... If you invest in 300 companies and they're selected relatively well... you're likely to have a very good IR

insights.claim: USVC runs a two-tier strategy: for early stage it backs the fund managers Nagpal judges best (since individual early companies are too numerous to pick), and only at later stage does it take positions in individual companies and use innovative structures like buying out LP stakes.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Shows where the fund claims edge (manager selection early, structure/access late) versus where it explicitly disclaims skill.
insights.quote: later stage I think you can start formulating a viewpoint on individual companies

insights.claim: USVC will only invest in a later-stage asset as part of a priced round led by an institutional lead, deliberately declining to set the price or terms and refusing to lead a Series C/D, because it lacks resources to diligence deep technical/scientific companies.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A 'draft off the institutional lead' guardrail; the fund is a scalable capital machine, not a technical underwriter, which caps both risk and edge.
insights.quote: we have a blanket rule that we will only invest in a later stage asset as part of a priced round by an institutional lead... where we're not going to set the price

insights.claim: USVC deliberately allocates almost 20% of the portfolio to brand-new funds that will not pay off for years, betting that the companies of the future will always be bigger than the companies of today even though private markets are 'very hot' now.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Signals long-duration exposure baked into the fund; near-term NAV may lag while these young funds mature, relevant for redemption timing.
insights.quote: we're taking almost 20% of the portfolio and putting it in brand new funds that will not pay off for quite a while

insights.claim: USVC's disclosed initial portfolio includes SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI, Sierra and Lora, with subsequently announced positions in Mercury and Supabase plus backed fund managers.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: The actual current holdings an investor buys exposure to; heavy AI/frontier-lab and fintech tilt.
insights.quote: we have SpaceX, we have Anthropic, we have OpenAI, we have Sierra, Lora... we've publicly announced investing in Mercury. We've publicly announced investing in Superbase

insights.claim: AngelList's manager-selection research favors a 'markups over baseline' metric, comparing an investor's markup rate against their cohort's average (while controlling for access to the biggest deals), because raw IRR is too skewed by a single outlier to be repeatable.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A quantitative screen for emerging managers that LPs can borrow; judge repeatability by cohort-relative markup rate, not headline IRR.
insights.quote: they introduced this idea of markups over baseline

themes.theme: Secondaries, stale marks, and NAV mechanics

insights.claim: USVC builds exposure by buying LP stakes in venture funds carrying inflated or stale (2021) marks, underwriting conservatively by assigning full value only to one or two prize assets and zeroing everything else.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A secondaries playbook for accessing hard-to-reach names cheaply; the conservative 'zero everything but the crown jewel' method is how they claim margin of safety.
insights.quote: there'll be one or two prize assets that I'm like... I'll strike a bunch of zeros and that's my fund math

insights.claim: Nagpal says every fund-secondary he has run finds at least ~10% of LPs willing to transact, which is all the liquidity USVC needs, even though many LPs resist because their dashboards still show old marks they believe are worth more.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Quantifies deal availability in the LP secondary market; a persistent ~10% seller pool means supply is reliable even in hot markets.
insights.quote: I've never done one of these where at least 10% of the fund has not been willing to transact and that's all I typically need

insights.claim: New sectors like physical AI and robotics structurally favor secondary buyers because most companies started in the last 4-5 years and their marks strike upward at each round, whereas 2018-2021 vintage venture funds hold marks that are on the way down.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Vintage and sector timing determine whether you buy at rising or falling marks; new-sector funds offer a favorable NAV tailwind.
insights.quote: if you're going and looking at... 2018 2019 2020 maybe even 2021 vintage venture funds there's a lot of companies in there that actually the old marks are on the way down

insights.claim: Large hedge funds have largely not yet entered the private-market secondaries space with a trader's mentality; Nagpal expects the 'financialization of everything' to bring them in, noting one ETF up 100x NAV couldn't be shorted because the borrow cost exceeded 100% of value.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Points to an under-competed arbitrage niche today and a likely influx of sophisticated capital later; early movers may capture the opacity premium before it compresses.
insights.quote: we don't actually see that many uh large hedge funds or those people playing here yet

insights.claim: Because USVC has pass-through taxation, end investors are eligible for QSBS, which can shelter up to $15M of gains tax-free when a qualifying C-corp sells, but only for portfolio companies that have raised under $75M and after a minimum ~3-year hold.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A tax edge specific to holding early-stage names through USVC; only the smaller current holdings qualify, and the benefit is back-loaded 3+ years.
insights.quote: because there's pass through taxation on USVC, the end investor is eligible for QPS... once a company raises over 75 million, they're no longer eligible

insights.claim: Nagpal recommends direct indexing over index funds because it delivers the same performance while generating usable tax losses to offset other gains.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A low-effort tax-alpha swap for the index core of a portfolio; harvested losses compound into meaningful savings over decades.
insights.quote: it's literally the exact same performance as an index fund, but you get usable tax losses that you can use to offset other gains

insights.claim: Nagpal suggests holding a Treasury money market fund instead of a high-yield savings account: yield is similar or slightly higher, and because the income comes from Treasuries it is exempt from state and local (e.g. New York City) taxes.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Immediately actionable cash-management tweak for high-tax-state investors; equal yield with a local-tax exemption.
insights.quote: instead of using your high yield savings account, you could potentially invest in a Treasury money market fund... but in New York, you also will not owe New York State, New York City taxes

insights.claim: For business owners Nagpal highlights stacking QSBS across multiple C-corps, converting an LLC earning six figures to an S-corp to avoid self-employment tax on the owner-distribution portion, and a solo 401k allowing up to a $72,000 deduction with any-asset investing and loans.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A concrete owner-level tax toolkit; the solo 401k (superior to SEP IRA for combined employee/employer contributions) is pitched as the most powerful retirement account for the self-employed.
insights.quote: the oneperson 401k... lets you get up to a $72,000 tax deduction, you can invest it in any asset class you want. It compounds taxfree

insights.claim: Nagpal attributes companies getting bigger faster (and dying faster) primarily to accelerating innovation rather than a macro dollar-debasement argument, framing USVC's bet as riding an exponentially faster product cycle.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Clarifies the fund's core assumption; if the driver is real innovation not monetary inflation, the thesis survives a stronger dollar but is exposed to faster obsolescence.
insights.quote: the cycle is spinning faster and faster. Companies are getting bigger faster. They also may die faster

insights.claim: Nagpal argues AI is no longer an investable category but a layer that inflects every company, analogous to how every S&P 500 company now has a tech component.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Cautions against 'AI' as a screening bucket; differentiate on what a company does, not whether it slaps AI on the narrative.
insights.quote: AI is no longer a category. AI kind of inflects everything

insights.claim: Nagpal sees the strongest current opportunities in companies tackling hard physical problems (energy, physical goods, the tooling and infrastructure behind foundation labs) rather than AI-wrapper software like an 'AI CRM', partly because hard problems attract talent and inspire fundraising.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A sector-tilt signal: deep-tech/infra over thin AI-application software; narrative-driven talent gravity is treated as a real competitive moat.
insights.quote: a lot of the companies right now that we're focusing on but also doing well, are those trying to tackle hard problems

insights.claim: Nagpal stresses that deviating from a disciplined indexing plan has repeatedly cost him, citing a time he parked cash for a 5% yield calling markets overheated instead of dollar-cost-averaging into the index as he advises others.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: Behavioral discipline reminder from a private-markets promoter: the base case is still automatic indexing, with private markets as the exception not the plan.
insights.quote: every time I've deviated from the plan, it's ended up being stupid

insights.claim: AngelList holds roughly $25 billion in assets on its platform and takes an economic stake (carry, direct investments) across the startup ecosystem, which Nagpal frames as an attractive index-like position on the private market itself.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Owning the platform that captures carry across thousands of deals is pitched as a diversified way to be long private markets; relevant to how USVC's edge is sourced from AngelList data.
insights.quote: 25 billion in assets on the west platform and compounding at a really fast rate

insights.claim: The public market is becoming a preferred wrapper for hard-to-access exposures, evidenced by fast-growing access ETFs, surging ADR demand, and an SK Hynix listing, paralleling what USVC does by offering a public proxy for private markets.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Signals a broader productization trend of packaging illiquid or foreign exposure into accessible vehicles; access, not just alpha, is the sellable innovation.
insights.quote: the DRAM ETF explode fastest growing ETF of all time... SKH Heinix is going to go and list this week

insights.claim: Nagpal singles out Naval Ravikant as a consistent outlier in AngelList's data, saying Naval's funds pretty reliably outperform, and credits him as an early backer of Nagpal's prior company Teachable.
insights.speaker: Ankur Nagpal (guest, USVC GP)
insights.so_what: A named manager the data flags as a genuine, repeatable outperformer; useful signal for who to follow or co-invest alongside in early-stage.
insights.quote: every time we look at any data, Naval is like an outlier since his funds pretty consistently outperform
